squat.camera_setup: Stand facing the camera or at a slight angle, 2-3 metres away. Full body from head to feet must be visible. A front or side angle both work well.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes slightly turned out
Keep your chest up and core braced throughout the movement
Push your hips back and bend your knees to descend
Lower until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor
Drive through your heels to return to standing
common_mistakes: Knees caving inward (valgus)
Excessive forward lean of the torso
Not reaching parallel depth
Rising onto toes instead of driving through heels
Rounding the lower back

lunge.camera_setup: Stand facing the camera or from the side. Ensure both legs are fully visible from hips to feet. Side angle gives the best depth assessment.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart
Step forward with one leg, lowering your hips
Both knees should bend to approximately 90 degrees
Keep your torso upright and core engaged
Push through the front heel to return to start
common_mistakes: Front knee extending past the toes
Leaning the torso too far forward
Back knee not lowering enough
Losing balance due to narrow stance
Uneven weight distribution

deadlift.camera_setup: Side view is best for deadlifts — camera at hip height, 2-3 metres away. Full body including hands and feet must be visible to assess back angle.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, bar over mid-foot
Hinge at the hips and grip the bar just outside your knees
Keep your back straight and chest up
Drive through your legs and hips to lift the bar
Stand tall at the top, then reverse the motion to lower
common_mistakes: Rounding the lower back during the lift
Bar drifting away from the body
Jerking the weight off the floor
Hyperextending at the top
Hips shooting up before the chest

bench_press.camera_setup: Position camera to the side of the bench at chest height. Both arms, shoulders, and the bar path should be visible. Front angle also works.
instructions: Lie on the bench with eyes under the bar
Grip the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width
Unrack and lower the bar to your mid-chest with control
Keep your elbows at roughly 45 degrees from your body
Press the bar back up to lockout
common_mistakes: Flaring elbows out to 90 degrees
Bouncing the bar off the chest
Uneven bar path or grip
Lifting hips off the bench
Not using a full range of motion

overhead_press.camera_setup: Stand facing the camera or from the side. Full body from feet to hands overhead must be visible. Step back if arms go out of frame at the top.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, bar at shoulder height
Grip the bar just outside shoulder-width
Brace your core and press the bar overhead
Lock out your arms at the top
Lower the bar with control back to shoulders
common_mistakes: Excessive lower back arching
Pressing the bar forward instead of straight up
Not achieving full lockout
Using leg drive (turning it into a push press)
Flaring the ribcage

pullup.camera_setup: Position camera facing you from the front or at a slight angle. Full body from hands on the bar to feet must be visible. Step the camera back far enough to capture full extension.
instructions: Hang from the bar with an overhand grip, slightly wider than shoulders
Engage your lats and pull your chest toward the bar
Pull until your chin clears the bar
Lower yourself with control to full arm extension
Avoid swinging or kipping
common_mistakes: Using excessive kipping or swinging
Not achieving full range of motion
Chin not clearing the bar
Not fully extending arms at the bottom
Shrugging shoulders instead of engaging lats

pushup.camera_setup: Place your camera so it can see your FULL BODY from head to feet. Best position: to the side at floor level, 2-3 metres away. A front or diagonal angle also works — just make sure shoulders, elbows, hips and ankles are all visible. Prop your phone on the floor or use a laptop at ground level.
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands slightly wider than shoulders
Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels
Lower your chest toward the floor by bending your elbows
Go down until elbows are at 90 degrees or chest nearly touches floor
Push back up to the starting position
common_mistakes: Hips sagging toward the floor
Hips piking up too high
Elbows flaring out to 90 degrees
Not going through full range of motion
Head dropping or looking up

plank.camera_setup: Side view is essential for planks — camera at floor level, 2-3 metres away. Your full body from head to feet must be in a straight line in the frame.
instructions: Start on forearms and toes, elbows under shoulders
Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels
Engage your core by pulling your belly button toward your spine
Keep your hips level - not sagging or piking
Breathe steadily and hold the position
common_mistakes: Hips sagging toward the floor
Hips piking up too high
Head dropping or looking up
Holding breath instead of breathing steadily
Elbows too far forward or backward

bicep_curl.camera_setup: Stand facing the camera. Arms from shoulders to hands must be clearly visible. Front angle works best for detecting upper arm movement and torso swing.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, arms at your sides
Hold dumbbells with palms facing forward
Keep your upper arms stationary against your torso
Curl the weights up by bending your elbows
Lower with control to full arm extension
common_mistakes: Swinging the body to generate momentum
Moving the upper arms/elbows forward
Not using full range of motion
Going too fast without control
Leaning back to lift heavier weight

tricep_dip.camera_setup: Side view or front angle. Arms, shoulders and torso must be visible. If using a bench, position camera so the full upper body and arms are in frame.
instructions: Grip the dip bars or bench with arms straight
Lower your body by bending your elbows to about 90 degrees
Keep your elbows pointing backward, not flaring out
Press back up to full arm extension
Keep your torso relatively upright for tricep focus
common_mistakes: Going too deep and stressing the shoulders
Flaring elbows out to the sides
Leaning too far forward
Not achieving full lockout at the top
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
